comments.text: Yesterday I signed a bill to return 3,400 acres of land to the Fon du Lac Band of Lake Superior Chippewa - a joint effort between the Tribe, the state, and the University of Minnesota.
A large stretch of forest that has been owned by the state of Minnesota for more than 100 years will soon be returned to the Fond du Lac Band of Lake Superior Chippewa.

Archaeologists Are Finding Dugout Canoes in the American Midwest as Old as the Great Pyramids of Egypt. In the waterways connected to the Great Lakes, researchers uncover boats that tell the story of millennia of Indigenous history. www.smithsonianmag.com/history/arch...

In the US, Catholic sisters return Wisconsin land to Ojibwe tribe in a historic first. The Franciscan Sisters of Perpetual Adoration have transferred a lakefront property to the Lac du Flambeau Band of Lake Superior Chippewa for the same $30,000 they paid in 1966.
buff.ly/iXOhxHj

This is a great piece that takes you deep into Native American Wisconsin, exploring the various tribes that lived around the Great Lakes, their canoe technology, and ancient forests and waterways. 🧪

Shout out to my husbands cousins who are direct descendants of Great Lakes Ojibway Chief Osaugie. Thanks #AntiquesRoadshow for highlighting the tribe’s art.

Two years ago, the Franciscan Sisters of Perpetual Adoration approached the Lac Du Flambeau Band of Lake Superior Chippewa, and the original caretakers of the land, with an unheard of proposition: Would they like a piece of their land back? 19thnews.org/2025/11/cath...

Nearly 90 acres is back under the control of the band, whose 47,000-acre reservation hugs the Lake Superior shoreline near the border of Ontario. It is considered the tribe’s largest land return in recent history, tribal leaders say.

"In the Great Lakes, it’s the oldest thing that’s ever been found.... "But with funding for the project soon expiring, ...[researchers] fear they could be forced to halt investigations into Michigan's submerged society before they’ve uncovered all of its secrets." www.bridgemi.com/michigan-env...

This is so cool: On Saturday, October 4th and Sunday, October 5th at the Detroit Film Theatre, A New Hope will be presented in Anishinaabemowin (Ojibwe), an original language of the Great Lakes region. Anangong Miigaading is part of an effort to preserve the language for future generations.

Ancient structures under Lake Huron reveal a 9,000-year-old submerged land bridge where Paleoindian hunters built stone lanes, camps, and tools to hunt caribou — offering rare, well-preserved evidence of early human life before rising waters formed the Great Lakes

I was glad to join the Bad River Band of Lake Superior Chippewa to unveil a new dual-language highway sign to properly recognize their sovereign territory. Folks, here in Wisconsin, we choose to embrace, not erase, our partnerships with Native Nations.

Aah Mahwi Nibi is now protected forever! This 29-hectare stretch of Lake Huron shoreline on the Saugeen (Bruce) Peninsula is home to rare habitats, clean water, and incredible biodiversity. Thanks to our supporters and partners, this land is safe for future generations. Learn more: brnw.ch/21wTsQF

An Anisininew (Oji-Cree) girl, likely from North Caribou Lake First Nation, wearing a hare-pelt coat. Photographed near Weagamow Lake, Ontario in 1956 by John Macfie. 📷 John Macfie | People of the Watershed exhibition (touring now in Parry Sound)

Wisconsin Historical Society, in partnership with Native Nations: Up to 11 canoes identified by archaeologists, earliest c. 4,500 years old, oldest in Great Lakes region. Additional evidence of submerged village site beneath Lake Mendota (Tee Waksikhominak)

“Norval Morrisseau is a reserve Indian. He is a trapper and handyman. He has a Grade 3 education. Sometimes he is an artist.” Shot at Sandy Lake, Ontario. From Marcel Carriére’s 1967 NFB documentary ‘The Indian Speaks.’ NFB Archives

I was honored to stand alongside the Fond du Lac Band of Lake Superior Chippewa, the University of Minnesota, and legislators as we transferred 3,400 acres of land back to its original caretakers. I’m grateful for the work we’ve done to strengthen Tribal-state relations.

5,200-year-old canoe in #LakeMendota rewrites #GreatLakes Indigenous life .. Beneath quiet water, a network once thrived. Boats weren’t personal property but shared tools, linking people, places, and beliefs. One unearthed canoe now helps piece together www.splashtravels.com/world-histor... History

It is not a kachina in the Hopi style, but more Great Lakes or Canadian. I think it’s an Ojibwe manitou, or meant to be in that style. The realistic animal head/mask and the clothing/adornment of the figure are more Ojibwe. This site has artisan examples. blog.myarthaus.com/manitous-kev...

Good evening from Grand Portage Bay on the north shore of Gichi Gami (Ojibwe- great sea), aka Lake Superior, at Grand Portage Natl Monument on Grand Portage Indian Reservation, reservation of the Grand Portage Band of Minnesota Chippewa Tribe The Great Northern Adventure #LandYacht #Trip10 Day 16

Tribal members in Minnesota and Wisconsin are working to revive the game of Baaga’adowewin, the Ojibwe version of lacrosse that once was a vital community-building tradition around the Great Lakes — but then virtually disappeared during the boarding school era.

My 4th-great grandfather signed the Treaty of 1854 which ceded Ojibwe land on the north and western shores of Lake Superior to the US Government. It also established reservations in Michigan, Wisconsin, and Minnesota.

At least 15 ancient dugout canoes have been found submerged in Lake Mendota in Madison, Wis. offering insight into cultural practice. One recent find is carbon dated @ 5200 years, one of the oldest in the Great Lakes region. 🏺 nativenewsonline.net/currents/sub...

Just found this great data source of Ojibwe names for features in MI/WI/MN. Gidakiiminaan (Our Earth), published by Great Lakes Indian Fish & Wildlife Commission. glifwc.org/publications... They also have a web map version: maps.glifwc.org

Hi there— I’m Anishinaabe from the northern shores of Lake Superior. Some call us Ojibwe or Chippewa. They didn’t kill us all. We’re still here, carrying our stories, languages, and traditions forward.

Soon after Lake Huron formed 9,000 years ago, the Alpena-Amberley Ridge was exposed as the lake levels were 100 metres below current levels. This allowed caribou to migrate through. These herds were followed by humans who had come to the area. 🧵 2/6

Underwater research is always like a needle in a haystack. AI can help. Recently, scientists built a virtual land bridge that helped them uncover a 9,000-year-old ancient caribou hunting site on Lake Huron. www.sciencefriday.com/segments/vir...

'Miigwech' means 'thank you' in the language of the Ojibwe (Anishinaabe) people. It expresses deep gratitude for kindness, nature and spiritual blessings. It's used widely as 'Chi-miigwech', acknowledging interconnectedness with all.

The human history of Lake Ontario dates back 11,000 years. Archeological evidence has found evidence of people who followed the caribou migration through the region. For the First Nations, the lake also provided fresh water, small animals and plant resources. 🧵 2/6
